title: Dog Head Pressing Against Wall: Causes and Emergency Triage
meta_desc: Is your dog pressing their head against a wall? This is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn what it means and why you need a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Head pressing is a serious neurological emergency that requires immediate veterinary care; taking a video of the behavior can help your vet provide a faster diagnosis.

What does it mean if my dog is compulsively pressing its head against surfaces?

Head pressing is a clinical sign where a dog compulsively pushes the top of their head against a wall, floor, or other hard surface. This behavior should not be confused with 'head butting' for affection; it is a neurological symptom indicating that the dog is in distress or experiencing pressure within the brain.

Is head pressing in dogs considered a medical emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-priority emergency. This behavior is typically a sign of serious neurological damage or dysfunction affecting the forebrain or brain stem.
  • Common underlying causes can include lead poisoning, liver shunts, brain tumors, or stroke.
  • If you observe your dog doing this, you must seek immediate veterinary care at an emergency animal hospital.

How can taking photos or videos of the behavior help my veterinarian?

  • Take a quick video or photo of your dog performing this behavior before heading to the clinic.
  • Dogs may stop head pressing due to the adrenaline or stress of a car ride, making it difficult to demonstrate at the hospital.
  • Visual evidence allows your veterinarian to confirm the symptom quickly and begin life-saving diagnostics without delay.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Neurologic signs such as head pressing may indicate a focal seizure or neuropathy. In older dogs, diseases affecting the CNS (e.g., tumors) or circulation (e.g., anemia, hypertension) may manifest as abnormal behaviors. If the animal is cyanotic and collapsed, especially with pawing at the face, consider upper airway obstruction and instruct the owner to perform a Heimlich maneuver or sudden chest compression to relieve the obstruction and manually clear the airway. If the animal is unconscious and not breathing, instruct the owner to perform mouth-to-nose resuscitation at a rate of 10 breaths/min.

Chapter: Neurology, Behavioral

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1562)

faqs.question: Is head pressing the same as a dog resting its head?
faqs.answer: No. Resting is a relaxed state. Head pressing is a compulsive, repetitive, or sustained behavior where the dog appears unable to stop pushing against a firm object.

faqs.question: Can liver disease cause head pressing?
faqs.answer: Yes. A condition called hepatic encephalopathy, which occurs when the liver cannot filter toxins from the blood, can lead to neurological symptoms including head pressing.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for circling, changes in vision, seizures, or sudden behavioral changes, as these often accompany head pressing in neurological emergencies.
